chunk_summary: Guidelines for managing vitamin D deficiency and rickets, including actionable 25(OH)D thresholds, treatment indications, and clinical decision-making pathways for children with suspected deficiency or related symptoms.
learning_objectives: Understand the serum 25(OH)D thresholds for vitamin D deficiency, insufficiency, and sufficiency.
Identify clinical scenarios where vitamin D testing and treatment are warranted.
Differentiate between biochemical low vitamin D and clinically significant disease.
Develop structured treatment and reassessment plans for rickets and vitamin D deficiency.
key_facts: Vitamin D deficiency is defined as 25(OH)D <12 ng/mL, insufficiency as 12–20 ng/mL, and sufficiency as >20 ng/mL.
Vitamin D toxicity is associated with 25(OH)D >100 ng/mL, often with hypercalcemia or hypercalciuria.
Testing for 25(OH)D should be reserved for high-probability cases where results will change management.
Clinical indications for treatment include bone pain, delayed milestones, fractures, or hypocalcemic symptoms, even without classic rickets deformities.
Routine screening of asymptomatic children is discouraged to avoid unnecessary high-dose regimens.
A structured reassessment checkpoint should be established at the start of therapy.
reference_quote: Order 25(OH)D when the probability of deficiency is high and the result will change your plan: suspected rickets, hypocalcemic seizures, recurrent fractures, chronic kidney or liver disease, malabsorption, long-term steroid or antiepileptic therapy, or severe immobility (for example, cerebral palsy).
document_key_topics: Vitamin D deficiency thresholds and treatment
